results.title: AI Agent for Job Search Review: Honest Results | Jerry Lee posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content:
The Dangerous "AI Loop" That’s Ruining Your Job Search 🚩
There is a frustrating cycle happening in the job market right now, and it’s costing talented people their dream roles. It looks like this:
The Misinterpretation: A candidate uses an AI tool to review their resume. However, AI often misreads complex formatting or lacks the context of specific industry niches, leading to incorrect feedback.
The Blind Edit: The candidate trusts the AI blindly and asks it to "fix" the resume based on that flawed review.
The Hallucination: The AI generates a new resume based on its own errors—adding irrelevant keywords or stripping away the human impact that actually gets you hired. [...] Sam Wright, graphic

I meet with job seekers all the time that try these mass apply tools and report that they haven’t yet gotten an interview after hundreds of applications. I’m all for a better match I’m not for fake marketing that wastes money

Jasmine Whittaker, graphic

I’ve heard reports (not verified) that some of the AI agents that apply for you are really bad. I’m talking: uploading blank documents for your resume, messing up basic identify information, and so much more.
So people think they are applying to 100s of jobs, and they never have a chance.

Alex Hills, graphic

Most “AI job search agents” are better at discovery than execution.
They can help you find roles, but real applications still need human judgment and accuracy.
Jerry Lee

Abdullah Imran, graphic [...] Agree & Join LinkedIn

# AI Agent for Job Search Review: Honest Results

View profile for Jerry Lee

“OMG I just found a AI Agent to Automate my Job Search”
Saw this all over my IG feed last month and was REALLY excited to try it.
Here are my results after I told it to apply to 5 jobs for me:
(honest review)
- Finds relevant jobs on Google: 9/10 
- Scores how well they fit you: 5/10 
- Builds a "customized" resume: 2/10 (rough) 
- Actually applies to jobs for you: 0/10 (it doesn't)
So no, an AI agent isn't landing you a job while you sleep (atleast not the ones I’ve seen).

results.title: AI Has Broken Hiring. Here’s How to Fix It.
results.content:
For the C-suite, this is no longer just an HR headache, it is a critical strategic risk. When the earliest filters in your hiring process stop working, your organization begins to systematically select for candidates who are best at performing the hiring process, rather than those best equipped to do the job. Left unchecked, this degrades a company’s overall talent density and compounds the cost of bad hires at scale.

## The True Cost of a Broken Hiring Funnel [...] ### Update later-stage interviews to reflect actual work.

Once authenticity is established, the goal is no longer to test who performs best in an artificial, no-tools environment. Later stages should allow candidates to use the same AI tools they would use on the job. Interviewers can provide flawed, AI-generated business plans, code snippets, or market analyses and ask candidates to evaluate them. This lets leaders assess judgment, the ability to spot hallucinations, identify strategic blind spots, and correct a model’s logic, one of the harder signals for AI to imitate in a live setting.

### Cultivate a culture of intellectual honesty over polished perfection. [...] While we treated the flags as indicators requiring further review rather than absolute proof of suspicious activity, we also found the behavioral patterns varied sharply by role type. For less technical roles, such as account executive positions, suspicion rates remained below 10%. However, mid-level technical roles reached close to 40%. In new graduate software engineering roles, suspicious patterns appeared in nearly 60% of sessions. The variation likely reflects two things. First, technical interviews often hinge on structured, objectively scored problems where AI can supply a clear answer in real time. Second, early-career candidates operate in tightly networked communities where tools, prompts, and tactics spread quickly. Even if many of these candidates weren’t using technology to

results.title: I built an AI Agent to handle all the annoying tasks I hate doing. Here's what I learned.
results.content: I decided enough was enough. I set out to build an AI agent specifically to handle this annoying, time-consuming crap for me. I decided to call him Pine (named after my street). The setup was simple: one AI to do the main thinking and planning, another dedicated to writing emails, and a third that could actually make phone calls. My little AI task force was assembled. Their first mission? Tackling my ridiculously high and frustrating Xfinity bill. Oh man, did I hit some walls. The agent sounded robotic and unnatural on the phone. It would get stuck if it couldn't easily find a specific piece of personal information. It was clumsy. But this is where the real learning began. I started iterating like crazy. I'd tweak the communication strategies based on its failed attempts, and crucially, I

results.title: Your AI Agent Fails 97.5% of Real Work. The Fix Isn't Coding.
results.content: more is being given to it. And in every case, the human who holds that context is the difference between the agent creating value and the agent creating damage. In some cases, existential damage. The market is really confused about this. On the one hand, we have the SaaS apocalypse. On the other hand, we have Gartner predicting in February that by 2027, half the companies that cut staff for AI will rehire workers to perform similar functions, often under different job titles. Their survey of over 300 customer service leaders found only 20% had actually reduced headcount because of AI and Forrester's data is even sharper. 55% of employers say they regret AI-driven layoffs. Do you see that talked about? We should probably talk about it. And yes, there are very public case studies where [...] that adopted generative AI saw junior employment drop roughly 8% relative to non-adopters within a year and a half. Senior employment kept rising. The decline hit hardest in AI-exposed occupation and critically, despite the headlines, was driven by slower hiring, not more firing. The conventional read here is that AI replaces junior workers. We've all seen a thousand headlines to say that. The better read is that AI replaces task execution. Juniors are hired for tasks, debugging, document reviews, first drafts, at least they used to be. They shouldn't be anymore. You should find better jobs for juniors and I have suggested a number of them in recent videos. But AI does these tasks adequately in isolation. Seniors survive because they provide something different. They hold the mental model [...] They cannot know the CMO made a promise to the CEO about a positioning shift that hasn't been written down anywhere. The agents will execute a technically strong campaign that reopens a wound the organization spent months healing. Or finance, agents can build technically perfect projections. They cannot know that certain numbers are politically dangerous internally even if they are technically correct. They can't read the room. They don't know what the board cares about this quarter versus last unless you tell them. In every case, the agent does the task well. It's not the issue. In every case, the agent cannot know whether this is the right task done the right way at this moment in this organizational context unless a whole lot more is being given to it. And in every case, the human who
